section: Public sector website accessibility should go beyond legal requirements
text: Date: Tuesday, 01st Apr 2025 | Category: Digital Marketing Emerging Safeguarding Issues Important Procedures Important Support Services Legal Frameworks Support and training Web Design & Development Public sector organisations have a vital role in ensuring that their websites and online services are accessible to all citizens. While compliance with legal requirements, such as the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) and the Equality Act, is essential, truly embracing digital accessibility goes beyond simply meeting these standards. It involves developing an inclusive culture, prioritising user experience, and ensuring that every individual can access vital services and information regardless of ability. Digital accessibility is about creating an environment where all users can navigate websites effortlessly, access information, and interact with services without barriers. This commitment reflects the values of equality and inclusivity and enhances the effectiveness of public services. When online platforms are designed with accessibility, they become more user-friendly for everyone, including individuals with disabilities, older adults, and those using various devices or internet speeds. To achieve meaningful digital accessibility, public sector organisations must adopt a proactive approach. This begins with understanding their users’ diverse needs and conducting thorough accessibility audits. Phew’s Website Design services assist public sector organisations in identifying accessibility gaps and implementing strategies to address them effectively. By leveraging tools and technologies that support inclusive design, organisations can ensure that their websites are usable by people with various disabilities, such as visual impairments, hearing difficulties, or cognitive challenges. Education and training are also crucial components of building accessible digital environments. Public sector teams must have the knowledge and skills to design and maintain accessible websites. Phew’s digital learning resources provide professionals with the latest insights and best practices in accessibility, empowering them to create solutions that meet the needs of all users. By fostering a continuous learning and improvement culture, organisations can ensure that accessibility remains a top priority in their digital initiatives. Moreover, engaging users in the accessibility journey is essential. Involving individuals with disabilities in user testing and feedback processes allows organisations to gain valuable insights into their websites’ real-world experiences. This collaborative approach enhances the quality of online services and demonstrates a commitment to inclusivity that resonates with the community. Ultimately, digital accessibility in the public sector is about more than just compliance; it’s about creating an environment where all citizens feel valued and included. By prioritising accessibility, public sector organisations can enhance service delivery, foster trust, and build stronger connections with their communities. Ready to elevate your digital accessibility efforts? Contact Phew to explore how our expertise in website design can help you create inclusive online experiences for all users.

text: Date: Tuesday, 08th Apr 2025 | Category: Audits Children's Services For Children’s Services teams, accurate data isn’t just about compliance – it’s about making informed decisions that protect vulnerable young people. Traditional spreadsheet-based audits often lead to time-consuming data collection and unreliable insights, preventing teams from focusing on what matters most. Using Phew’s online audit tool , as Sam Anderson from Dudley Safeguarding People Partnership explains, “the Partnership is able to monitor the effectiveness of local safeguarding arrangements from a single agency and a multi-agency perspective. They can also promote high standards of safeguarding work and foster a culture of continuous learning, improvement, and opportunity for challenge process across the partnership.” The impact is significant: organisations report up to a 250% increase in response confidence and save four weeks of administrative time annually. One partnership discovered their manual Section 175 audit, estimated at 54 hours, actually took 97 hours – highlighting the hidden costs of traditional methods. Sarah Slater, Manager of the Policy, Improvements and Partnership Team, confirms the benefits: “Using the Phew Audit System has reduced the resources required, both to deliver the audit by the business support team and to contribute to the audit by partner agencies, which is much appreciated by everyone where resources are limited and often stretched.” With features like automated data collection, real-time reporting, and secure collaboration tools, organisations can focus on analysing trends and implementing improvements rather than managing spreadsheets. The system enables teams to coordinate multi-agency audit and quality assurance activity easily, while maintaining the ability to report progress and exceptions via performance dashboards. The results speak for themselves: up to 120% increase in completions year-on-year and 48% of license costs recouped through time savings. Would you like to find out more about how Phew’s Audit Syste m can improve your data accuracy and streamline your audit processes? Email hello@phew.org.uk to learn more about transforming your approach to safeguarding audits.

text: Date: Thursday, 03rd Apr 2025 | Category: Audits Important Child Protection Terms Safeguarding Building strong safeguarding partnerships is crucial for ensuring the safety and wellbeing of children and young people. Collaborative efforts among various stakeholders—including local authorities, schools, and safeguarding agencies—are essential to create a comprehensive support network. One powerful tool for enhancing these partnerships is an effective auditing system that streamlines data collection and transforms it into strategic insights. The shift from traditional methods, such as spreadsheets, to modern audit systems allows organisations to assess safeguarding practices comprehensively. Phew’s Audit System exemplifies this transformation, enabling education settings and their partners to understand their strengths and areas for development like never before. Charles Daniels, Schools Improvement Officer at Staffordshire County Council, states, “We have achieved our initial objectives and many more. The Phew Audit System has allowed us to understand strengths and areas for development in our education settings in a manner that we have never achieved before. It has provided us with the best value in all areas.” This innovative system empowers organisations to triangulate safeguarding information, fostering enhanced communication and collaboration among partners. Providing a centralised data analysis platform opens a dialogue with education settings that may have previously had limited contact. As a result, engagement with safeguarding services increases, creating a more integrated approach to child protection. The ability to analyse and share safeguarding data is particularly important for identifying trends and areas that require attention. With Phew’s Audit System, stakeholders can monitor compliance with safeguarding policies and procedures, ensuring every child receives the necessary protection. This comprehensive view supports continuous improvement and helps organisations allocate resources effectively, driving better outcomes for children. Moreover, modernising safeguarding audits allows for real-time feedback and insights, critical for swift decision-making. By using data-driven approaches, safeguarding partnerships can react promptly to emerging issues, ensuring timely and effective interventions. The emphasis on collaboration and shared knowledge leads to a culture of transparency and accountability among all partners, reinforcing their commitment to safeguarding. Ultimately, the journey from spreadsheets to strategic insights marks a significant advancement in safeguarding partnerships. By embracing innovative auditing systems like Phew’s, organisations can build stronger, more resilient networks dedicated to the safety and well-being of children. Ready to enhance your safeguarding partnerships? Contact Phew to discover how our Audit System can support your mission and transform your approach to safeguarding.

section: Key Recommendations for Safeguarding and Health Websites
text: Educate stakeholders : Ensure boards and commissioners understand why reported numbers appear lower than reality, using citations from industry research and the ICO’s own experience Adjust KPIs : Set targets based on trends and percentages rather than absolute visitor numbers Document your methodology : Include standard caveats in all reports explaining the cookie consent impact, citing: The ICO’s 90.8% drop Advance Metrics’ finding that only 25.4% accept all cookies Industry average drops of 15-60% Invest in qualitative measures : User surveys, feedback forms, and case studies provide context that numbers alone cannot Consider cookieless analytics : Explore server-side analytics options that respect privacy while providing basic metrics Maintain transparency : Be open about data limitations while emphasising the importance of respecting user privacy

section: Understanding Your True Website Impact: Why Your Analytics Only Tell 30% of the Story
text: Date: Tuesday, 16th Sep 2025 | Category: Legal Frameworks Web Design & Development A guide for safeguarding and health organisations on accurate impact reporting in the age of cookie consent The Hidden Challenge in Your Website Reports If you’re responsible for reporting on your safeguarding or health website’s performance, you’ve likely noticed something concerning: your visitor numbers dropped dramatically when cookie consent became mandatory. At Phew, we observed traffic reductions ranging from 20% to 60% across our client websites when the Privacy and Electronic Communications Regulations (PECR) and UK GDPR enforcement tightened – findings that align with industry research showing similar drops across European websites ( LinkedIn, 2023 But here’s the crucial insight: your website didn’t lose these visitors. You lost visibility of them. What Exactly Are Cookies? Before diving deeper, let’s demystify cookies for non-technical users: Cookies in Simple Terms cookie is a small text file that websites place on your visitor’s device (computer, phone, or tablet) to remember information about them. Think of cookies like a cloakroom ticket at a theatre: When you arrive (visit a website), you’re given a ticket (cookie) The ticket has a number that identifies your coat (your preferences and actions) When you return, the theatre uses that ticket to remember which coat is yours (the website remembers who you are) Types of Cookies Essential Cookies : Like remembering what’s in your shopping basket – the website can’t function properly without these. Analytics Cookies : Like a visitor counter at a museum – they track how many people visit, which exhibits they view, and how long they stay. Marketing Cookies : Like a shop assistant who remembers what you looked at last time and suggests similar items. The crucial point? Under PECR and UK GDPR, only essential cookies can be placed without permission. Everything else – including the analytics cookies that power your reports – requires explicit consent. Understanding the Cookie Consent Impact What Actually Happened? When the Information Commissioner’s Office (ICO) strengthened enforcement of PECR and UK GDPR requirements, websites must now: Ask for explicit consent before placing analytics cookies Make refusing as easy as accepting (no pre-ticked boxes or hidden decline options) Continue providing full service to users who decline cookies The result? According to CookieYes’s 2025 Global Compliance Guide, fewer than 25% of users in countries like Germany and France accept cookies , while the UK picture aligns more closely with these European patterns than with the USA, where acceptance rates vary widely depending on the study methodology. The ICO’s Own Experience Perhaps the most striking example comes from the ICO itself. After implementing their own best practices in 2019, their website traffic dropped by 90.8% – from 119,417 users per day to just 10,967 ( Matomo, 2020 Industry-Wide Evidence Our observation of significant traffic reductions is validated by extensive research: The Advance Metrics study (2018-2023) found that 76% of visitors ignored cookie banners entirely in 2018 , though this dropped to 33.6% by 2023 ( Advance Metrics, 2024 Only 25.4% of users accept all cookies on the first level of the cookie banner ( Advance Metrics, 2024 A scientific study reports that websites experience an average 15% traffic reduction long-term following GDPR implementation ( ScienceDirect, 2022 An etracker study found that with legally compliant design, an average of 60% of visit data is lost etracker, 2024 UK-Specific Data Recent UK studies reveal interesting patterns: 56% of UK consumers always accept cookies without thinking, according to a Thales study ( Marketing Tech News, October 2023 However, 18% actively reject cookies daily , with this rising to 23% among 45-54 year olds (Nano Interactive, 2023) In France, approximately 28% of users consistently provide consent for cookies ( Statista, 2022 Who Are You Actually Measuring? This creates an interesting bias in your data. The visitors who accept cookies tend to be: More digitally confident users Those who spend longer on your site More likely to be professionals rather than vulnerable service users Regular visitors who trust your organisation This means your analytics might over-represent engaged professionals while under-representing the vulnerable populations you serve. How Analytics Actually Work (In Simple Terms) Think of website analytics like a visitor book at a building entrance: Without cookies : It’s like having a security camera that only counts people entering – you know someone visited, but not who or what they did inside. With cookies : It’s like giving visitors a temporary badge that tracks which rooms they visit, how long they stay, and whether they’ve been before. But now, 70% of visitors politely decline the badge. What We Can and Can’t Measure Without cookie consent, we still know: Overall page load numbers (though less accurate) Which pages are accessed most Download counts for documents General traffic patterns We lose visibility of: Individual user journeys Returning vs new visitors Accurate unique visitor counts Detailed demographic information Cross-device usage The 2025 Regulatory Landscape ICO’s Current Enforcement Campaign The ICO is taking unprecedented action in 2025: 134 of 200 UK websites reviewed were found non-compliant and issued warnings ( ICO, January 2025 The campaign is expanding to the top 1,000 UK websites The ICO is exploring AI tools to automatically detect non-compliant cookie banners Of 53 organisations contacted in 2024, 38 quickly adjusted their banners to comply Key Compliance Issues Identified The ICO found widespread problems including: Dropping cookies before consent No “Reject All” option or making it less prominent than “Accept All” Pre-ticked consent boxes Vague or misleading wording No way to withdraw consent once given Reporting Impact Accurately: A New Framework 1. Apply the Research-Based Multiplier Caveat When presenting analytics data, always include this context: “These analytics represent approximately 25-30% of total website visitors who accepted cookies, based on industry research (Advance Metrics, 2024). Actual figures may be up to 3-4x higher. The ICO’s own website experienced a 90.8% reduction in recorded traffic after implementing compliant cookie consent (ICO, 2019).” 2. Focus on Trends, Not Absolutes Instead of reporting “We had 1,000 visitors,” report: “Recorded traffic increased 15% month-on-month” “Document downloads show consistent growth” “Peak access times remain consistent at 10am-2pm” 3. Complement with Non-Cookie Metrics Strengthen your impact reporting with: Server-side analytics (counts page loads without cookies) Document download logs (usually tracked without cookies) Contact form submissions Email newsletter sign-ups Social media engagement Key Events & Data Points Early 2020: Pre-enforcement period. Most sites using implied consent or no banners. Estimated 85-90% traffic visibility. Oct 2020: ICO publishes new cookie guidance requiring explicit consent. Many sites begin implementing compliant banners. April 2021: Google Analytics changes: tracking no longer triggered by default. Must have explicit opt-in. Major drop in recorded traffic begins. 2022-2023: Advance Metrics study: Only 25.4% accept all cookies. 33.6% ignore banners completely (down from 76% in 2018). 2024: ICO intensifies enforcement. 38 of 53 contacted sites update banners. Studies show 10-15% “normal” drop with proper implementation. 2025: ICO targets top 1,000 UK websites. 134 of 200 reviewed found non-compliant. Average visibility estimated at 30-40% of actual traffic. Monthly and Annual Reporting Best Practices Monthly Reports: Operational Focus Track trend changes rather than absolute numbers Monitor which resources are most accessed Identify content gaps through search terms Flag technical issues (broken links, slow pages) Annual Reports: Strategic Impact When justifying budgets and demonstrating impact: Do: Show year-on-year trend improvements Highlight qualitative feedback alongside quantitative data Include case studies of website impact Compare your trends to sector benchmarks (with caveats) Reference external research to validate your methodology Cite studies like the ICO’s 90.8% drop to contextualise your data Don’t: Present raw numbers without context Compare pre-GDPR to post-GDPR absolute figures Assume your measured audience represents all users Making Peace with Privacy-Respecting Analytics Alternative Solutions Some organisations are exploring: Privacy-focused analytics tools like Matomo that offer cookieless tracking options Server-side analytics that don’t require client-side cookies Google Consent Mode which provides modelled data for users who decline cookies (though this requires specific thresholds to be met) However, even with privacy-focused tools, the fundamental challenge remains: respecting user choice means accepting partial visibility. Expected vs Actual Impact Industry guidance suggests ( getterms.io, 2025 Normal drop : 10-15% for properly configured banners If traffic dropped 95% : Your setup is likely incorrect HubSpot reports : Almost 50% of users don’t interact with banners at all Key Recommendations for Safeguarding and Health Websites Educate stakeholders : Ensure boards and commissioners understand why reported numbers appear lower than reality, using citations from industry research and the ICO’s own experience Adjust KPIs : Set targets based on trends and percentages rather than absolute visitor numbers Document your methodology : Include standard caveats in all reports explaining the cookie consent impact, citing: The ICO’s 90.8% drop Advance Metrics’ finding that only 25.4% accept all cookies Industry average drops of 15-60% Invest in qualitative measures : User surveys, feedback forms, and case studies provide context that numbers alone cannot Consider cookieless analytics : Explore server-side analytics options that respect privacy while providing basic metrics Maintain transparency : Be open about data limitations while emphasising the importance of respecting user privacy The Bottom Line Your website is likely reaching and helping far more people than your analytics suggest. When only 25-30% of visitors accept tracking cookies (based on the Advance Metrics study), you’re seeing just the tip of the iceberg. This doesn’t diminish your impact – it means you need to be smarter about how you measure and report it. Remember: in safeguarding and health services, the most vulnerable users are often the least likely to accept tracking. Their privacy matters, and respecting it while still demonstrating your service’s value requires a nuanced approach to impact reporting. Need Help Reporting Your Website’s True Impact? Struggling to demonstrate your website’s real value when analytics only show 30% of your visitors? At Phew, we specialise in helping safeguarding organisations, Multi-Academy Trusts, and public sector clients develop robust reporting frameworks that account for cookie consent challenges. We can help you: ✅ Develop accurate methodologies for the “invisible” 70% ✅ Create compelling reports combining all data sources ✅ Implement privacy-respecting analytics solutions ✅ Ensure full ICO compliance Get in touch today and we’d be happy to discuss how we can help you report your website’s true impact with confidence. Contact Our Team Book a Free Consultation Further Reading and Resources Key Studies Referenced: ICO Online Tracking Strategy 2025 Advance Metrics Cookie Behaviour Study (2024) etracker Cookie Consent Benchmarks ICO Cookie Guidance Tools and Solutions: Matomo Analytics – Privacy-focused analytics platform Google Consent Mode This guide is for informational purposes and does not constitute legal advice. Organisations should consult with legal professionals for specific compliance requirements.

text: Date: Tuesday, 28th Oct 2025 | Category: Client Multi-Academy Trusts Phew news Safeguarding Phew Achieves ISO14001 Certification Phew , our commitment to safeguarding children has always been our driving force, but we believe that care should extend to the world they live in, too. That’s why we’re proud to confirm that Phew is now ISO14001 certified. Cert No: 24358 What is ISO14001 Certification? ISO14001 is an internationally recognised environmental management standard. It’s awarded to organisations that actively work to reduce their environmental impact and operate sustainably. For us, this means aligning our internal processes with wider global efforts to protect the environment, without losing focus on the core of what we do: safeguarding people. Our Journey to Certification Becoming ISO14001 certified meant reviewing and improving how we manage resources, energy use, and waste. We made small but significant shifts in how we operate, from streamlining digital infrastructure and travel to improving supplier engagement. Our team embraced these changes not just as a requirement, but as a reflection of our values. You can learn more about our approach to compliance, governance, and due diligence on our dedicated page. What This Means for Our Clients For our clients, including Multi-Academy Trusts, schools, and safeguarding organisations, this certification is another signal that we’re a company you can trust. It shows that we don’t just talk about doing the right thing – we prove it, through independent verification and accountability. Being ISO14001 certified demonstrates that we are forward-thinking, responsible, and committed to operating with integrity across the board. Looking to the Future Looking ahead, we’ll continue to find new ways to help our clients safeguard both children and the environment – because we believe those two things go hand in hand. Environmental sustainability is increasingly recognised as a safeguarding issue, affecting the wellbeing and future security of young people. If you’d like to work with a supplier who takes every aspect of responsibility seriously, we’d love to hear from you. Get in touch to find out how Phew can support your organisation’s needs while maintaining the highest standards of environmental and corporate responsibility.

section: Why Phew Websites Outperform Standard Public Sector Builds
text: Date: Thursday, 23rd Oct 2025 | Category: General Insight Article Legal Frameworks Multi-Academy Trusts Strategy & Analysis Support and training Web Design & Development Public sector organisations need websites that do more than simply inform – they must engage citizens, simplify processes and safeguard sensitive information. At Phew, we’ve honed a formula that consistently delivers these outcomes. Here’s how our approach sets you up for lasting success. Built‑in Accessibility and Compliance From day one, every Phew website is developed to meet the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) and the Public Sector Bodies (Websites and Mobile Applications) (No. 2) Accessibility Regulations 2018 . We layer automated scans and manual checks into our process so you’ll spot potential issues long before a Government Digital Service (GDS) spot‑check . That means fewer surprises, fewer emergency fixes – and every citizen, regardless of ability, can navigate your site with ease. Rock‑Solid Security and Privacy Handling safeguarding data demands the highest level of protection. Our infrastructure is certified to ISO 27001 for information security and fully compliant with the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) , ensuring that documents, case notes and personal information stay confidential. Regular penetration tests and security audits keep you ahead of emerging threats, so you can focus on service delivery, not system vulnerabilities. Content Management, Simplified Our custom content management system (CMS) takes the pain out of publishing. You don’t need coding skills to add a new policy page, upload a PDF or embed training materials – our intuitive interface guides you through every step. Role‑based access controls let you grant permissions to different team members, and built‑in versioning keeps a clear history of every update. As one partner saw, document downloads jumped by 950 percent once their team could publish resources instantly. Seamless Ecosystem Integration Your website never lives in isolation. Phew sites connect effortlessly with your existing learning management system (LMS) and audit tools, creating a unified experience for staff and external partners. Whether you’re rolling out a new training module or generating audit reports, everything flows through one secure portal. No more duplicate data entry, no more branding gaps – just smooth workflows that respect your organisation’s unique requirements. Data‑Driven Decision Making Knowledge is power, and our monthly analytics reports put it in your hands. You’ll see which pages are most visited, which resources are downloaded most often and where users drop off. Armed with these insights, you can refine content, improve navigation and prioritise investments where they matter most. In one case, an organisation achieved a 485 percent boost in overall site sessions within six months simply by acting on the trends our reports revealed. text: At Phew, safeguarding the data of our clients, employees, and stakeholders is paramount. We are dedicated to upholding the confidentiality and security of the information entrusted to us for processing. In light of the increasing prevalence of data breaches worldwide and evolving compliance regulations, we continuously assess and enhance our data protection measures. To ensure compliance with relevant data protection laws, Phew conducts thorough due diligence and adheres to various regulatory and legal frameworks, both mandatory and voluntary. We also actively engage with accreditation schemes to reinforce our data security commitment. Below, we outline the methods through which we prioritise data protection and compliance.

text: ISO 14001:2015 is the internationally recognised standard for Environmental Management Systems (EMS), providing a framework for organisations to design, implement, and continually improve their environmental performance. This standard helps us minimise our environmental footprint, comply with relevant legal requirements, and achieve our environmental objectives. ISO 14001 helps control environmental aspects, reduce impacts and ensure legal compliance, supporting our commitment to sustainable operations and resource efficiency. You can view our certificate here ISO 14068-1:2023 is the latest international standard for carbon neutrality management, replacing the previous PAS 2060 standard. This standard provides principles, requirements, and guidance for achieving and demonstrating carbon neutrality through quantifying, reducing, and offsetting carbon footprints, utilising a hierarchical approach that prioritises direct and indirect greenhouse gas emission reductions over offsetting. The standard ensures that carbon neutrality efforts are true, fair, science-based and transparent, providing a structured approach that supports the journey to carbon neutral/net zero. You can view our report here Carbon Neutrality represents our commitment to achieving net-zero carbon emissions by balancing the greenhouse gases we emit with equivalent amounts removed from the atmosphere. This involves adopting sustainable practices, working with sustainable suppliers, investing in emission reduction technologies, and supporting verified carbon offset projects to minimise our net contribution to greenhouse gases in the environment. Our carbon neutrality accreditation demonstrates our proactive approach to climate action and environmental responsibility.

text: Phew is committed to providing a web site that is inclusive and available for all user groups including the disabled, the visually impaired and those with motor deficiencies and cognitive disabilities. We have made sure that, wherever possible, pages comply with the key requirements of the W3C (World-Wide Web Consortium) best practice accessibility guidelines. We are actively working to improve the site so that every page will conform to the AA level of the WCAG 2.0 (Web Content Accessibility Guidelines).

text: At Phew we aim to provide world-class customer service and place the client at the heart of everything we do. In line with our main guiding principle, to commit and deliver with integrity, our staff are dedicated to exceeding standards of customer care through continuous improvement and close collaboration with our clients. To provide the best level of client care we promise to: Be honest, understanding and approachable at all levels of client communication Explain any technical aspect of work in clear English, ensuring both parties have a full understanding of any agreement made Attend all meetings on time, prepared with relevant documents and equipment, dressed in a professional manner Respect client confidentiality for all information we receive during the project process, with full adherence to GDPR regulations Be efficient, precise and effective during all aspects of the project Ensure all projects are completed to meet both industry and client standards Assign each project an Account Manager and Project Manager to ensure professional project management at all stages Follow all elements of our quality assurance process Meet agreed deadlines and inform the client of key milestones (to be agreed with client) Listen to client feedback and use it to constructively improve our processes Maintain client contact after project completion to suggest new products or services that could benefit them Apologise, give a full explanation and rectify the situation when an error occurs Aim to respond to all calls, emails, messages and any other communication as promptly as possible Be as helpful as possible when a query is raised and if we cannot assist, we will find a member of staff who can Any verbal discussions regarding any future, ongoing or past projects will be documented and emailed for confirmation, this includes communication via phone and email Log any calls or meetings on our internal system under the relevant account, opportunity or project with notes so all communication is documented This policy is communicated and understood within the company and is reviewed periodically to ensure it remains suitable for our needs. Salma Miah Business Support Manager Issued: 23rd August 2022

text: The DDSCP, formed in 2019, works in partnership with the statutory partners and other relevant agencies, such as health providers, education settings and probation, to improve local safeguarding arrangements and hold each other to account as to how they are keeping children safe. The Partnership is made up of sub-groups to carry out its work, including Learning and Development, which provides multi-agency training for practitioners across Derby and Derbyshire. Due to the large geographical area and the impact of a global pandemic, the ability to coordinate training courses across the agencies was proving challenging. The partnership also wanted to improve on the ability to report on the impact of training on practice and improved outcomes for children, which had been limited on a previous learning management system.
page_title: Derby & Derbyshire Safeguarding Children Partnership | Phew Case Study
meta_description: How Derby & Derbyshire Safeguarding Children Partnership increased both delegate bookings by 630% and course offerings by 360% using Phew’s LMS.

text: Shortly after adopting the LMS, the pandemic began and it was not possible to run face-to-face courses as planned. The Partnership had to quickly adapt to a virtual training offering, which would have been much more difficult without the system already in place. DDSCP were able to offer a suite of eLearning courses, which had not previously been available. DDSCP have also managed to successfully increase the number of practitioners able to access multi-agency training, and currently have more than 9000 delegates registered. They are able to offer more in-depth reporting to statutory partners, including detailed qualitative analysis, which is used to triangulate the impact on practice, alongside the work carried out by policy and procedures and key findings from quality assurance audits and learning reviews. The ability to use anonymised evaluations to track stakeholder satisfaction and the embedding of skills into practice, has led to improved outcomes for children. The Phew Learning Management System has allowed us to build an authentic learning platform which delivers and tracks learning in one place, whilst allowing us to respond quickly to organisational changes and provide in-depth analysis to partners. It gives us the opportunity to create and support a culture of learning with increased flexibility to meet the needs of our stakeholders, regardless of location. Sarah O’Brien, Senior Learning & Organisational Development Officer at Derby and Derbyshire Safeguarding Children Partnership

section: A sector-leading flexible Online Audit System
text: Having worked with the safeguarding sector since 2007, Phew understands the specific needs and challenges faced by Children and Adult Boards or Partnerships , as well as Multi-Academy Trusts Children’s Services Leveraging our experience and expertise gained through close client relationships, we have developed an innovative, time-saving integrated Audit System, designed to support local and regional safeguarding audits, including Sections 11 and 175/157. Our Live Safeguarding Audit solution includes real-time tracking, continuous updates, and improved oversight – ensuring that safeguarding audits remain dynamic, responsive, and effective throughout the year. National Safeguarding Audit Survey Phew conducted a nationwide survey on children’s safeguarding auditing, inviting children and joint safeguarding partnerships to provide their insights. The survey delved into key aspects, including the frequency of Section 11, Section 157/175, and Case Audits, the tools used for distribution and analysis, and the primary challenges faced. Read the National Safeguarding Audit Survey report here

section: A sector-leading flexible Online Audit System
text: Having worked with the safeguarding sector since 2007, Phew understands the specific needs and challenges faced by Children and Adult Boards or Partnerships , as well as Multi-Academy Trusts Children’s Services Leveraging our experience and expertise gained through close client relationships, we have developed an innovative, time-saving integrated Audit System, designed to support local and regional safeguarding audits, including Sections 11 and 175/157. Our Live Safeguarding Audit solution includes real-time tracking, continuous updates, and improved oversight – ensuring that safeguarding audits remain dynamic, responsive, and effective throughout the year. National Safeguarding Audit Survey Phew conducted a nationwide survey on children’s safeguarding auditing, inviting children and joint safeguarding partnerships to provide their insights. The survey delved into key aspects, including the frequency of Section 11, Section 157/175, and Case Audits, the tools used for distribution and analysis, and the primary challenges faced. Read the National Safeguarding Audit Survey report here

page_title: Dudley Safeguarding People Partnership | Website Case Study
meta_description: How Dudley Safeguarding People Partnership increased visitor sessions to their new website by 485% and the number of information downloads by 950% in the first six months of implementing Phew’s website framework.

text: Dudley Safeguarding People Partnership (DSPP) is responsible for making sure children, young people and adults are kept safe in the borough. DSPP work with relevant agencies to improve local safeguarding arrangements and hold each other to account in order to protect Dudley’s most vulnerable people. Their previous website was owned by the Local Authority, and as a multi-agency partnership, they were keen to show their independence. Feedback from users of the site found that it was not easy to read, and it was time-consuming and difficult for administrators to make continuous updates. The team at DSPP were also keen to integrate their Phew Learning Management System with the new website so they could promote courses with a consistent look.
page_title: Dudley Safeguarding People Partnership | Website Case Study
meta_description: How Dudley Safeguarding People Partnership increased visitor sessions to their new website by 485% and the number of information downloads by 950% in the first six months of implementing Phew’s website framework.

text: The new website was designed specifically for their stakeholder groups and the the areas are differentiated visually with one colour for adults section and another for children. DSPP have access to flexible layouts which provide a streamlined and refreshed feel for users. Information and guidance to professionals and members of the public is now easier to find than ever before, and DSPP have seen an increase of 950% in document downloads from their website within the first six months of launching. The website was designed with the latest compliance to industry benchmarks, including accessibility, and the team at DSPP are able to continue to monitor this inhouse. Along with their Learning Management System being seamlessly integrated with the website which provides a consistent look and feel, they are also able to send out their newsletter to stakeholders using the website content management system. A monthly detailed analytics report which highlights how their content is performing is sent to the team, and this provides them with an overview of their website which they’ve never had before. “We turned to Phew when we identified the need for a new website, primarily because we were already utilising two of their systems, which consistently prove effective, and we held their client service in high regard. The website’s adaptability, adherence to industry standards, and seamless integration with our Learning Management System has significantly enhanced our online presence. “The website now serves as a comprehensive platform for professionals seeking quick, user-friendly access to information. With the added benefit of self-monitoring accessibility and streamlined newsletter distribution through the content management system, our online operations have become more efficient than ever. Phew’s monthly detailed analytics reports further provide us with valuable insights into our website’s performance.” Sam Anderson, Safeguarding Partnership Business Manager

text: Welcome to the Phew Safeguarding Glossary – your comprehensive guide to essential safeguarding terminology and practice. This extensive resource provides clear, authoritative definitions for over 200 key terms used across children's and adults' safeguarding, from fundamental concepts like child protection and multi-agency working to emerging challenges such as contextual safeguarding and online safety. Navigate easily through our complete collection using the A-Z filter for quick alphabetical browsing, or explore by categories such as 'Most Important, High Searched Terms', 'Legal Frameworks' and 'Emerging Safeguarding Issues' to focus on your specific area of interest. Each entry not only explains what terms mean but demonstrates how Phew's innovative digital solutions support safeguarding professionals in addressing these critical areas through our Learning Management Systems, Audit tools, and compliance frameworks. Whether you're a Designated Safeguarding Lead seeking clarity on statutory requirements, a safeguarding partnership coordinating multi-agency training, or a professional working to protect vulnerable individuals, this glossary connects theory with practical, proven solutions. For deeper insights into current safeguarding challenges and innovations, explore our recent analysis of the future of safeguarding training trends for 2025 , discover how training directly impacts children's well-being , and learn about supporting the updated Working Together guidance through effective multi-agency collaboration. text: HSCP is a statutory body that leads on keeping children safe and ensuring their wellbeing in the local authority area of Hampshire. The Partnership includes local agencies who work together to ensure that child protection services in Hampshire are effective and keep children safe. The Partnership has a learning and improvement framework shared across organisations working with children and families in Hampshire, linking quality assurance to learning and improvement. The challenge was that HSCP used a fragmented system of spreadsheets and ledgers to administer event bookings and management services. This process was vastly inefficient as the information would quickly become dated.
page_title: Hampshire Safeguarding Children Partnership | LMS Case Study
meta_description: How HSCP increased knowledge from training by 20% and have had more than 15,300 course attendees, since using Phew’s Learning Management System.

text: The simple display ensures administrators and system users can efficiently use all the site’s functionalities with minimal training. Popular features include the LMS’ comprehensive reporting which provides fingertip access to invaluable statistics and impact evaluations, along with time-saving features such as only having to list an event once. The LMS has provided many time, cost and resources efficiencies that have added to the increased value of the Partnership. One of the successes of the system is that HSCP has upgraded the LMS system to accommodate more of their significant training provision. The success of our virtual courses wouldn’t have been possible without Phew’s Learning Management System and eLearning module. In 2019-20 we had approximately 435 eLearning delegates, which increased considerably to 2,100 in 2020-21. By March 2022 we had reached 6,000. The Phew Learning Management System makes processes robust, simpler, and more efficient by automating what were previously manual processes. It also allows us to measure the effectiveness and productivity of each event or course effortlessly, as well as report the impact on children. Sam Smith, Partnership Manager at Hampshire Safeguarding Children Partnership

text: The Modern Slavery Act 2015 is an Act of the Parliament of the United Kingdom. It is designed to combat modern slavery in the UK and consolidates previous offences relating to trafficking and slavery. The act extends essentially to England and Wales, but some provisions apply in Scotland and Northern Ireland.

text: Phew has a zero-tolerance approach to modern slavery and are committed to acting ethically and with integrity in all our business dealings and relationships. We do not enter into business or partnerships with any organisation, which knowingly supports, or is found to be involved in slavery, servitude or forced or compulsory labour. As an equal opportunities’ employer, we’re also committed to creating and ensuring a non-discriminatory and respectful working environment for our people. Our recruitment and people management processes are designed to ensure that all prospective employees are legally entitled to work in the UK. Phew is committed to complying with relevant employment legislation and codes of practice as a minimum benchmark. Wherever possible we strive to exceed legislative requirements by developing policies and procedures that help it achieve its aim of being an Employer of Choice.

text: The Managing Director has overall responsibility for complying with legal and ethical obligations and for investigating allegations of modern slavery in the Company’s business or supply chains, along with the assistance of the Business Operations Manager. If a staff member believes or suspects a breach of The Modern Slavery Act 2015 has or may occur, they must notify the Managing Director as soon as possible. We are committed to ensuring no one suffers any detrimental treatment or victimisation because of reporting in good faith their suspicion that modern slavery is or may be taking place in any part of the business or in any of its supply chains. Phew employs its staff on standard contracts of employment where each is free to terminate in accordance with the agreed terms and laws. The conditions of employment are laid down in the staff handbook. Further information regarding the Modern Slavery Act 2015 can be found on the UK government website.

text: We recognise that it is important for you to understand how we use your personal data. Therefore, please read the following information carefully as it contains important information regarding the Phew Design Limited website and the way in which we use your personal data. This Privacy Statement explains how we use personal data about visitors to our website, including customers and potential customers and about individuals that get in contact with us, our suppliers and those individuals whose personal data we otherwise process in the course of our business. This statement also covers data processing related to job applications and our recruitment activities. We will only use personal data in ways that are described in this statement and only ways that are consistent with our obligations and your rights under applicable data protection laws. Who we are? For the purpose of applicable data protection laws, the data controller (in other words, the organisation that determines how and for what purposes your personal data is used) will be Phew Design Limited (‘Phew’), a company with registered number 04219588 and address Cheribourne House, 45a Station Road, Willington, Bedfordshire, MK44 3QL Our Data Protection Officer can be contacted at accounts@phew.org.uk All handling of your personal data is done in compliance with the Data Protection Act 2018, the General Data Protection Regulation (EU) 2016/679 and the UK GDPR (together, the “Data Protection Legislation”). The terms “Personal Data”, “Special Categories of Personal Data”, “Personal Data Breach”, “Data Protection Officer”, “Data Controller”, “Data Processor”, “Data Subject” and “process” (in the context of usage of Personal Data) shall have the meanings given to them in the Data Protection Legislation. “Data Protection Lead” is the title given to the member of staff leading our data protection compliance programme in lieu of a requirement for a Data Protection Officer. . What are your rights? When reading this statement, it might be helpful to understand that your rights arising under Data Protection Legislation as set out below. If you would like to exercise any of those rights (or if you have any queries about this policy or the way that we use your data, please contact us at accounts@phew.org.uk RIGHT DESCRIPTION OF RIGHT Access A right to access personal data held by us about you Rectification A right to require us to rectify any inaccurate personal data held by us about you Erasure A right to require us to erase personal data held by us about you. This right will only apply where (for example): we no longer need to use the personal data to achieve the purpose we collected it for; or where you withdraw your consent if we are using your personal data based on your consent; or where you object to the way we process your data (in line with your right to object, below) Restriction In certain circumstances, a right to restrict our processing of personal data held by us about you. This right will only apply where (for example): you dispute the accuracy of the personal data held by us; or where you would have the right to require us to erase the personal data but would prefer that our processing is restricted instead; or where we no longer need to use the personal data to achieve the purpose we collected it for, but you require the data for the purposes of dealing with legal claims Portability In certain circumstances, a right to receive personal data, which you have provided to us, in a structured, commonly used and machine-readable format. You also have the right to require us to transfer this personal data to another organisation, at your request Objection A right to object to our processing of personal data held by us about you where the processing of such data is necessary for the purposes of our legitimate interests, unless we are able to demonstrate, on balance, legitimate grounds for continuing to process personal data which override your rights or which are for the establishment, exercise or defence of legal claims Not to be subject to automated processing A right for you not to be subject to a decision based solely on an automated process, including profiling, which produces legal effects concerning you or similarly significantly affect you Withdrawal of consent and objection to marketing A right to withdraw your consent, where we are relying on it to use your personal data (for example, to provide you with marketing information about our services or products). You can object to direct marketing at any time You can also gain access to your personal data by emailing accounts@phew.org.uk with the subject line: “Subject Access Request”. When you submit a ‘subject access request’, you will need to provide confirmation of your identity. The data you requested will be provided free of charge and our response will be made within thirty (30) days unless our Data Protection Officer deems your request to be excessive or unfounded. If this is the case, we will inform you of our reasonable administration costs in advance and/or any associated delays, giving you the opportunity to choose whether you would like to pursue your request. If you believe we have made a mistake in evaluating your request, please see the section ‘Who can you complain to?’. If you have questions about any of the rights mentioned in this section, please contact our Data Protection Officer at accounts@phew.org.uk Personal data we may collect from you and how we collect it Personal data you provide to us directly Ways in which you might provide the data to us This is personal data about you that you give us, which can happen in a wide variety of ways, including by creating an account with us via HubSpot to provide you with products and services and your use of those products or services, signing up to our newsletter, corresponding with us by e-mail, telephone or SMS or if you mention or interact with us on social media (for example by mentioning/tagging us or by contacting us directly). It also includes personal data you provide when you enter one of our competitions or promotions, if you complete one of our customer satisfaction surveys, provide us with feedback on our products, search for a product on our website, report a problem with our website or products, enter into a contract with us or otherwise provide us with any personal data. What type of data might be included? The personal data you give us may include but is not limited to: your name, postal address, e-mail address and phone number (including mobile number), gender (and preferred salutation), date of birth, social media handle and personal data on your social media account, financial and credit card personal data, purchase history (and sales history if you are a distributor or a reseller), thoughts about our products (including complaints), and any content relevant to entering one of our competitions or promotions (which may include a photo). If you are one of our suppliers, we will process your business contact details and your job role. We will only ever ask you to give us personal data which we need in order to provide you with the products or services that you have requested from us. Job applicants If you apply for a role with us, we will collect a range of information directly from you either by email, over the phone or via an online job application process/system which may include but is not limited to: your name, address and phone number (including mobile number), email address, gender (and preferred salutation) date of birth, your health information (if relevant to your application), the contents of your CV (including details of previous roles, any relevant qualifications you have obtained and any languages that you speak), evidence that you are legally permitted to work in the country you are applying for a role in, your current and desired annual salary, your current notice period, candidate portfolio, whether you have previously been employed by us , how you heard about the role you are applying for and any other information that we reasonably require as part of the recruitment process. If you are a job applicant and want more detail about how we process your personal data as part of our recruitment process, you can contact our HR department by email at hello@phew.org.uk Personal data we collect or generate about you When you visit our website or get in touch with us, we may collect, generate, store and use certain personal data about you. This personal data may include: technical information, including the Internet protocol (IP) address used to connect your computer to the Internet, your login information (if accessing an account with us), browser type and version, time zone setting, browser plug-in types and versions, device types, operating system, time and date of consent and platform and any phone number used to call our customer service number. It may also include information about your visit to our website, including the full Uniform Resource Locators (URL), clickstream to, through and from our site (including date and time), products you viewed, searched for or purchased, page response times, download errors, length of visits to certain pages, page interaction information (such as scrolling, clicks, and mouse-overs) and methods used to browse our website. This information is used by us to administer our website and for internal operations, including troubleshooting, data analysis, testing, research, statistical and survey purposes and for the control of illegal acts. We reserve the right to check these log files and data retroactively if there is reasonable suspicion of an illegal act due to concrete indications. We safeguard your interests by anonymising your IP address upon collection so that you are not identifiable. Personal data we receive from other sources We may occasionally receive personal data about you from other sources, for example if you leave a review on our third-party consumer review platform or use any of the other websites we operate or the other services we provide. What are the lawful bases for processing personal data? Under Data Protection Legislation, there must be a ‘lawful basis’ for the use of personal data. The lawful bases are: ‘your consent’; ‘performance of a contract’; ‘compliance with a legal obligation’; ‘protection of your, or another’s vital interests’; ‘public interest/official authority’; and ‘our legitimate interests’. We are committed to being transparent about our data processing activities. If you have questions about the specific legal basis we are relying on to process your personal data, please contact our Data Protection Officer at accounts@phew.org.uk What are Phew’s ‘legitimate interests’? Legitimate interests are a flexible basis upon which the law permits the processing of an individual’s personal data. To determine whether we have a legitimate interest in processing your data, we balance the needs and benefits to us against the risks and benefits for you of us processing your data. This balancing is performed as objectively as possible by our Data Protection Officer. You are able to object to our processing and we shall consider the extent to which this affects whether we have a legitimate interest. If you would like to find out more about our legitimate interests, please contact accounts@phew.org.uk About our processing of your data We might collect, use, store and transfer different kinds of Personal Data about you which we have grouped together as follows: Identity Data such as names, usernames or similar; marital status; title; date of birth; sex and gender. Contact Data such as addresses; email addresses and telephone numbers. Financial Data such as bank account and payment card information. Transaction Data such as information about payments and details of purchases you have made. Technical Data such as IP addresses; login data; browser info; time zone; location; browser plug-ins; operating systems; platforms and other technology on the device used to access this website. Profile Data such as usernames; passwords; security answers; purchases/orders; interests; preferences; feedback and responses to surveys, blogs and messages. Usage Data such as analytics relating to how you use the website. Marketing and Communications Data such as your preferences about receiving communications from us or third parties. Special Categories of Data such as details about race or ethnic origins, religious or philosophical beliefs, sex life, sexual orientation, political opinions, trade union membership, information about your health, genetic or biometric data. Children This Website is not directed toward children (as defined by local law), nor does Phew knowingly collect information from children without parental consent except where in compliance with applicable law. We may also collect, use and share Aggregated Data such as statistical or demographic data. Aggregated Data can be derived from your Personal Data but is not itself Personal Data as it cannot be used to reveal your identity. If Aggregated Data is ever used in combination with your Personal Data and becomes identifiable, it will be treated in accordance with this notice. How do we use your personal data and what are our justifications for doing so? Whenever we use your personal data, we need to make sure that we have established a valid legal justification (known as a “lawful basis”) for that use of data. We have described the ways that we use your data and the associated lawful basis below. Reference What categories of information about you do we process? Why are we processing your data? Where did we get your personal data from? Client Correspondence and Professional Services · Identity Data · Contact Data We use the personal data you have given us in order to communicate with you and provide our goods and services to you. This can also include processing data in relation to support. This processing is conducted lawfully on the basis of ‘performance of a contract’. Obtained directly from you. Direct Marketing · Identity Data · Contact Data · Marketing and Communications Data We use this personal data in order to market our products and services to you that we believe you will benefit from. This processing is conducted lawfully on the basis of ‘our legitimate interests’. Obtained directly from you. Legal Obligations · Identity Data · Contact Data To ensure we comply with any legal and statutory obligations that might arise. This processing is conducted lawfully on the basis of ‘compliance with a legal obligation’. Directly obtained from you. Product Hub Account · Identity Data · Contact Data · Technical Data · Profile Data To provide you with access to your account. Directly obtained from you. Enquiries · Identity Data · Contact Data · Any other unsolicited personal data that you choose to submit We use the contact information sent to us through web forms, by phone or by email in order to respond to enquiries from existing and potential clients. This processing is conducted lawfully on the basis of ‘our legitimate interests’. Directly obtained from you.

meta_description: This Privacy Policy sets out the legal basis on which any Personal Data that we collect from you, or that you provide to us, will be processed by us.

text: Third-Party Links Our services may include links to third-party websites or applications. Interacting with these may allow them to collect or share your data. We don’t control these third-parties and aren’t responsible for their privacy practices. Once you leave our site, our privacy policy doesn’t apply. We recommend reading the privacy policy of every site you visit. Contact them directly if you have concerns about their data practices. Security We take the security of your personal data very seriously and have put physical, technical, operational and administrative strategies, controls and measures in place to help protect your personal data from unauthorised access, use or disclosure as required by law and in accordance with accepted good industry practice. We will always keep these under review to make sure that the measures we have implemented remain appropriate. Unfortunately (and as you will probably already know) the transmission of information via the internet is not completely secure. Although we will do our best to protect your personal data, we cannot guarantee the security of your data transmitted to our Global Patient Survey; any transmission is at your own risk. Disclosure of your information We may share your Personal Information with our group members, including subsidiaries and partners, as defined in the UK Companies Act 2006. As part of GDPR compliance, we ensure any third-party recipient respects data protection standards. We may disclose your data: when buying or selling a business or assets; if our company or majority of assets are acquired to fulfil legal obligations; to enforce agreements; or to protect the rights, property, or safety of our company, customers, or others. What happens if I refuse to give Phew my personal data? The information about you that we have collected for the performance of our contracts is required in order for us to successfully fulfil our obligations to you. If you choose not to provide the personal data requested, we will not be able to enter into a contract with you to provide the services we offer. If we are already processing your personal information under a contract, you must end our contractual relationship (as/where permitted) in order to exercise some of your rights. If we process some personal information as part of a contractual relationship with a Data Controller, then any requests to restrict this type of processing should be forwarded to the Data Controller; they will be responsible for discussing your concerns and making any decisions. What profiling and automated decision making does Phew perform? Phew does not use your personal data for automated decisions or profiling. All decisions involving your data are made by our staff, not algorithms. We treat users as individuals, avoiding assumptions based on profiling. We aim for fairness and transparency in processing your data while respecting your privacy. For any queries, reach out to accounts@phew.org.uk How long will your data be kept? Phew holds different categories of personal data for different periods of time. Wherever possible, we will endeavour to minimise the amount of personal data that we hold and the length of time for which it is held. If ‘consent’ is the basis for our lawful processing of your data, we will retain your data so long as both the purpose for which it was collected, and your consent, are still valid. Occasionally, we might identify a legitimate interest in retaining some of your personal data that has been obtained by consent. If we do, we will inform you that we intend to retain it under these conditions and identify the interest specifically. If we process your data on the basis of ‘legitimate interests’, we will retain your data for as long as the purpose for which it is processed remains active. All categories of personal data that are held by us because they are essential for the performance of a contract, will be held for a period of six years, or as otherwise required under applicable law, for the purposes of exercising or defending legal claims. Who else will receive your personal data? Phew may pass your data to the third parties listed in the section ‘Third Party Interests’ below. Who else will receive your personal data? Third party suppliers and service providers involved in our contractual relationship with you: Like most businesses, we work with third party suppliers and service providers as part of the day-to-day operation of our business. Some of these trusted suppliers will process your personal data on our behalf and provide services to us such as research, analytical health data, statistics. We will always make sure that we require them to meet agreed standards for the protection of your data and they will only ever be allowed to use the data in order to provide services to us and not for their own commercial purposes. If any of these trusted third-party suppliers is based outside of the UK or EEA (i.e. in a territory where local laws may not provide the same level of protection for your data), we will implement safeguards to ensure that your personal data is protected in accordance with our obligations under data protection law. Phew may pass your data to the third parties listed in the section ‘Third Party Interests’ below. Where your personal data may be processed We may transfer your personal data outside of the UK, Switzerland and the European Economic Area (“EEA”) where local laws may not provide legal protection for your personal data in the same way as is applicable in the UK, Switzerland or the EEA. Examples of when your personal data may be processed outside of the UK, Switzerland and the EEA include for the purposes of for example research and analysing health data. Similarly, some of our trusted third-party suppliers may transfer data outside of the UK/Switzerland /EEA and wherever this is the case, we will implement appropriate safeguards to protect your personal data. Whenever we send (or permit a third party to send) your personal data outside of the UK, Switzerland and the EEA, we will make sure that we take steps necessary to protect your data as required by applicable laws. For example, we may require the overseas recipient (including any of our group companies) to enter into particular contract terms such as the European Commission’s Standard Contractual Clauses or the UK IDTA. Does your data leave the EU? Yes. Details are included in the section ‘Third Party Interests’ below. Phew has risk assessed where Personal Information may be transferred outside the UK and EEA. As part of our own due diligence, we have identified that some of the Personal Data held for and by Phew resides outside of the EU. Phew will continue to monitor this for Phew considering any third-party provider changes in the future. Should a requirement for data to be transferred outside of the UK and/or EU in future, Phew will implement controls and safeguards to ensure that equal to or greater data protection measures are enforced and records retained to evidence this. In the interests of providing a quality service, we may use providers located in the United States. These providers are bound by the contractual provisions of the EU Commissions model clauses and UK model clauses. page_title: Somerset Safeguarding Children Partnership | Phew Case Study
meta_description: How Somerset Safeguarding Children Partnership reduced training administration time by 85%, whilst increasing the number of delegate bookings by 230%, using Phew’s Learning Management System.

text: The SSCP holds the responsibility for the oversight of multi-agency partnership safeguarding arrangements, which includes the dissemination of learning and information to all agencies and individuals who come into contact with children. All training and events were organised through a manual system which was immensely time-consuming and severely restricted any expansion of the training offer. Learning opportunities were also offered by partner agencies, however there were no effective means to coordinate and signpost to them, resulting in a fragmented training offer and practitioners often unaware that quality opportunities existed, or being informed of them in an ad-hoc manner.
page_title: Somerset Safeguarding Children Partnership | Phew Case Study
meta_description: How Somerset Safeguarding Children Partnership reduced training administration time by 85%, whilst increasing the number of delegate bookings by 230%, using Phew’s Learning Management System.

text: SSCP immediately saw results once they adopted the Phew LMS. It had a profound effect on the ability to disseminate learning across all agencies, as well as leading to greater capacity and creativity in doing so. Reporting through the LMS has also proved to be highly effective through its easily accessible format which allows targeted information to be instantly available and highlights areas of further partnership development, which previously would have entailed a laborious manual trawl in order to obtain accurate information. The implementation of the Phew LMS has completely transformed the ability to disseminate learning across the Partnership, brought about closer agency collaboration, and enabled the expansion and coordination of training across the county. The ability of the Phew team to listen and respond to ideas and suggestions ensures that the system is continually developed, which results in a comprehensive training offer without the need for additional capacity or significant investment. Steve Macabee, Multi-Agency Training Manager at Somerset Safeguarding Children Partnership
